pattern: Two arrays represent the same text as long as the characters line up in order once every fragment is glued together — the way the text is chopped into pieces doesn't matter.
hints: Forget the array boundaries entirely; only the final sequence of characters matters.
Concatenate every string in each array in order, then you just have two strings to compare.

You can compare while walking both arrays with a pair of pointers (fragment index and offset within fragment) to avoid building huge strings, but a straightforward join-and-compare is also correct within the given limits.

edge_inputs.description: same text split into different fragment boundaries
reversed split pattern of the same pair
one array is a single long string, the other many short fragments summing to the same content
concatenations differ by exactly one trailing character
same characters and same total length but in a different order
single-character arrays on both sides, equal
arrays at the length cap of 100 single-character fragments each

stress.description: build a random base string, then split it two different random ways into fragment arrays
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    total_len = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    alphabet = 'abcde'
    base = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(total_len))
    def split_randomly(s):
        pieces = []
        i = 0
        while i < len(s):
            step = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(20, len(s) - i)))
            pieces.append(s[i:i + step])
            i += step
        return pieces if pieces else ['']
    word1 = split_randomly(base)
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        word2 = split_randomly(base)
    else:
        mutated = list(base)
        idx = rng.randrange(len(mutated))
        mutated[idx] = rng.choice([c for c in alphabet if c != mutated[idx]])
        word2 = split_randomly(''.join(mutated))
    return [word1, word2]
stress.note: half the time splits the identical base string a second way (must be True), half mutates one character first (must be False)
